title: The Irish Book Lover, vol. II, no. 8, March 1911

THE

JAMES GODKIN.

Godkin was a very interesting figure in Irish Literary life in th.
middle of last century, though to many he 1s only known as the father:

of cne of the most brilliant publicists of our time, Edwin Lawrence
Gcdkin, editor of the New York ‘‘Nation.” James Godkin was born at
Gorey, Co. Wexford, in 1806. A member of his family, writing in the

“ British Weekly,” 27th June, 1907, states that ‘‘ in his teens he had
made a brief excursion into the Catholic Church, to which he did not be-
long, and from which he soon returned.” On 3rd September, 1834, he was |
ordained to the pastorate of the Congregational Church, meeting in the -

building known as ‘‘ The Tabernacle,”” College Street, Armagh, and
afterwards he became a general missionary of the Irish Evangelical
Society. Gavan Duffy says that when he went to Belfast in 1840 to edit
the ¢ Vi'ndicatm' '’ he found Godkin there as an Indepéﬁdent‘Minister :
“ We met at first as opponents, but on better acquaintance chscovered
We had much in common, and finally became assocmtes.
Towards the end of 1844, under the nom de’ guerre of ““A I’rouestant
~ Clergyman ,”) he took third prize, £50, in an essay competmon pmmated
b}’ the Repeal Association. In the * Advertisement ”’ thereto he ad-
voca,tes an Irish Parliament only on the following condltlons- ‘““ First,
- that it can be obtained by peaceful and constltutmual means ; secondlv,
th&t the Irish Prouestft,nts, generally, shall concur in demfmdmg it ;
5 thlrdly, that mvmlable security be given that ne1tlier ’ohe Church of
;‘t; Romg noy any other Church shall ever be establlshed or endowed m
thlﬂ country.” - He ends his prefa,torv remarks by descnbmg his esszw
sl . ﬂﬂ a hearty defence af lns beloved countrv, a.nd expresses hls wﬂlmgness :

he  was  dismissed from his missionary duties, but Dgyig
procured him employment in the ‘ Kreeman’s Journal’ office,
and hoped to get him appointed a professor in the new colleges,?
(Dufty.) This plan falling through, Godkin went to London, gaynq
became a leader-writer for provincial journals, Irish and Scottish, anq
a contributor to the reviews and magazines. Returning to Ireland ip
1849 he established in Belfast the ** Christian Patriot >’ (Is anything
now known of this journal?), and on its failure, he proceeded to Derry to
take up the editorship of the ‘‘ Standard,’” published in that city. In
the troublous times of 1850-54, he was one of the leaders in the League
of North and South, established by Duffy. Subsequently he was editor
of the Dublin ‘¢ Daily Express,’”’ for ten years, and Irish correspondent
of the ‘ Times’’ for seven yvears. His latest and finest journalistic
enterprise was the establishment of ‘‘ The National Review ”’ in Dublin
on 1lst June, 1868. This was commenced as a monthly of 24 pages,
folio, price 3d., and was intended to do for Irish opinion what the
‘“ Spectator ”’ and ‘‘ Saturday Review ”’ then did for English. ™The
tumes seemed propitious for such a journal. The Reform Bill had added
immense numbers to the electorate, the Irish Church and Land had
been marked out as subjects for reform, and the hopes of the Irish
Liberals ran high. With the fourth number it became a weekly and
the price reduced to 2d. But in spite of all its cleverness and ability,
- when the general election was over, its work seems to have been done,
~and 1t died with the closing week of 1868. Its most notable contribu-
tion was ‘‘The Battle Fields of ’98,”” by the editor, in which he says, ‘I
was born near one of the battlefields. I rambled over many of them in
my ydliﬂf. I have lately met an old hook published by the Government,
~and containing a lisv of protestant families who had obtained compen-
sation for losses inflicted by the rebels during the war. The list -
cluded ten families of my own name in one district.”” Tt contained

much tradition carefullv collected, and included several unpubhshed'
documents of grea,t interest. He had intended re-publishing iv, for an

"advt appears announcmg ag < Preparmg for publication, Nmeiv-elgllb

puring his journalistic career, he published several historical com-
pilations which gave evidence of marvellous industry in the collection
of data, great skill in marshalling facts, and vigour and pungency in
driving home arguments. In 1873 he obtained a civil list pension from
the Gladstone Government, and died on 23rd May, 1879.

120 THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.

PRINTING IN MULLINGAR, 1773-1825.

In the following list of printing in Mullingar we find g greater
number of items than in any other town recently dealt with. William
Kidd, who appears vo have been the first printer, was either g Dublin
man or at least carried on printing in Dublin for many years and appears

I have
shown in the list that the Westmeath Journal was the first newspaper

to have moved to Mullingar and carried on the business there.

to be published there, and that it certainly began some years earlier
than has hitherto been supposed. In preparing this list I was assisted
by Mr. James Tuite, of Mullingar,
knowledge, and one of the contributors to this Journal, who has glven

a local antiquarian of considerable

me such notes as appear throughout it, and to whom 1 wish to express
my indebtedness. One is struck by the character of the books Kidd
printed in Mullingar. They represent what might be called Puritan
theology. No doubt there was a demand for such there at the time.
Kidd appears to have been succeeded by Wm. Thomas Kidd, whose
name first appears 1n 1816, and some years later on we find I'rancis
Kidd, who possibly was a son or grandson of William Kidd the first
printer.
1773 The Westmeath Journal (?) [See unden 1783].
1780 or 1781 The Westmeath Journal. (?) [See under 1783].
1782, March and April The Westmeath Journal. [Vide Wilson’s
‘“ Collection of the Resolutions of the Volunteers, etc.,”
Dublin, 1782].

The Westmeath Journal. (W Kidd.) [Vide Maddeﬂ:
Vol. IL, p. 243].

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.

« POETS WHO HAVE PASSED AWAY.”

Bernard \Iagenms, who died in Dublin on the 5th of January, 1911,
was a brother of Mrs. Ellen Iorrester, the poetess, and therefore uncle

: Artbur M. Forrester, ¥anny Forrester, and May Forrester, all of
wlmm are recorded in the *‘ Poets of Ireland.” They were originally from
Co. Monaghan. Bernard Magennis was born at Ballybay in February,
1833, and was at first a national teacher. He wrote an enormous
amount of verse for Irish, American, English, Catholic and Temperance
journals. I know at least three volumes of his verse—‘‘ The Red Hand
and other Poems,”” Dublin, 1888, * Anti-Humbug,”’ a satire, Manches-
ter, 1890 ; and ‘‘ The Catapult,”” a satire, Dublin, 1897.

Patrick F. Durkan, author of ¢ Poems,” Tuam, 1862, and of many
pieces of verse in Western Irish and Dublin papers in the seventies and

eighties of last century (generally over the signature of ‘“ A Swinford

Boy ’’), died at Scranton, Pa., U.S.A., early in November, 1910. He
was a professor in a college at Scranton, and published various poems
in the Pennsylvanian papers. In ‘“ The Poets of Ireland,” I wrongly
gave his death as occurring at Swinford, Co. Mayo. in December, 1893,
but it was a namesake of his, and probably a relation, who died on that
daze.

Peter Magennis, the ‘‘ Bard of Derrygonnelly,” ched on September
16th, 1910, at the great age of 93. Like his namesake, just mentioned,
(who was not related to him in any way), he was| also a national school i
teacher. He was born near Derrygonnelly (where he died) and lived | 3
there all his life. His books are, ° Poems,” Enniskillen, 1844; Rt
“‘ The Ribbon Informer,”’ a tale of Lough Erne, 1874 ; *“ Tully Castle,
tale of 1641, 1877 ”’; ¢ Poems >’ Enniskillen, 1388; another edltlon of‘.‘“"'

'-William [11.”’ It is very well done, and the number of works described
indicate wide reading and indefatigable research.
I note that ‘“ The New Ireland Review " ceases publication with

the current number. It has been in existence for seventeen years, and

brightest intellects in Ireland have contributed articles dealing 1in
scholarly fashion with every topic of popular concern, and its passing
nence can only be regarded with sincere feelings of regret.

tu the British Colony, and to that part of 1t called the Oligﬂrehy |

or Ascendancy ; but this is to say that he came of the race whjey sup.
plies ‘* National Ireland ” with most of its heroes and prophets: he wyg
a Protestant of the landlord class. In one of these volumes therg js an
essay, ‘‘ In a Landlord’s Garden,”” in which he speaks of the tragedy
not of the Kelt, but of his own race in Ireland. *‘ The broken gre.
houses and mouse-eaten }_ibmries that were designed and collecteq by
men who voted with Grattan are perhaps as mournful in the end 4
the four mud walls that are so often left in Wicklow as the only rep.
nants of a farmhouse.”

Synge had no prejudices, and no one knew his politics.  He came
to Ireland at a time when the demand for a national literature was, as
someone wittily said, still confused with tha demand for Irish soap and
boot polish. Bright boys from the leagues that organise Irish prejudices
tried to break up the Abbey Theatre on the occasion of the production
of ¢ The Playboy of the Western World ’* ; Synge said during vhe week
of rioting, ‘‘ It’s an extravaganza,” and then relapsed into silence.

SELECTIONS FROM ANCIENT IRISH POETRY, translated by Kuno Meyer
(Constable.) This collection, which is classified under various heads,
i¢ preceded by a scholarly and interesting introduction which traces the
originals before the Viking raids of the eighth, and the rich harvest of

the eleventh, century onwards. The distinguished translator confessiis
| nders 1t

interesting to compare them in their unadorned heauty, with the poetiC

paraphrasings of Mangan and Ferguson. The originals will not suffer

by the comparison. The book is heautifully turned out.

FORTHCOMING WORKS.

Mr. Edward Arnold announces a new work from the accomplished
pen of Mr. Joseph R. Fisher, B.A., editor of the ‘‘ Northern Whig,”
dealing with the last thirty years of the 1rish Parliament. Mr. Fisher
has devoted much time and research to the preparation of his materials,
and we anticipate a scholarly account of this very interesting period in
our country’s annals.

Mr. Elliot Stock has almost ready the concluding volume of ‘‘ The
History of Wexford,’”” by Philip H. Hore. A labour of more than thirty

acquisition of materials is thus fittingly brought to a conclusion. All
the castles, seats and places of historical interest in the north of the
county are noticed in this volume. O’Donovan’s survey letters are in- S
cluded as well as contemporary illustrations by Wakeman and Du L

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. | 131
IRISH LITERARY SOCIETY.

on Wednesday, 25th January, Mrs. E. M. Field gave a most Inter-

-

§ asting Tea Table Talk on the Hedge School. After reviewing Carleton’s

. ccount of his experiences, and suggesting that with his native instinct
ace

{0 I
| -"tiﬂlls: the speaker gave a short historical review of the rise, growth, and

Suelay A

ke the best of a story, he had somewhat over-coloured his descrip-

M—

decay of the Hedge School. The earliest attempt at State education
| as in 1537, when the Irish Parliament introduced National education.
The bill had, of course, to be approved by the English Privy Council,
«lich insisted that ‘* the English tongue, habit and order be henceforth
and without ceasing,” established in Ireland. The navural result was
! the encouragement of the illiciv Hedge School education. In 1570 Queen
' Flizabeth established Protestant schools, and Campilon testifies that
though monasteries were silent and schools swept away ‘‘ yev the pea-
sants speak Latin like their vulgar tongue.” And in later times Sir

William Petty writes that the poorest have a good knowledge of French

—

' aud Latin, especially in Kerry. For the more remote the district, the
easier hecame the work of the poor hunted Hedge school master. Under
the Penal Laws any Catholic found teaching could be fined £20. As .
informers got £10 for finding a schoolmaster, and it is on record that | )

boodhounds have been used to ‘trace them, the profession was a risky
one. The Hedge School, so called originally from béing held in the J_
open air in the shelter of a hedge, had often to take a refuge in a hole |
Wnderground, or in a rude turf shelter in a bog. One or two boys wouid ,
b posted as sentries while the rest worked. All through the 18th cgﬁ'—

e L T B e e B | f

twry there were constant reports to Government, that barefooted boys 2
o the hills of Connemara or in Leinster, talk better Latin than the .

An interesting ‘‘ History Night > was held on the 22nq l‘ebruqry L.
when Dr. Miller Maguire lectured on ‘ The Maguire Clan.” )y 0 H, f
O’Donnell taking the chair. Dr. Maguire traced the general bearin.g 0[.-"
the tribal system on the history of Ireland, and succeeded in lllustmtm e
from the record of his own clan several important historical points. lhf-.'

i -

Maguires, whose chief traced descent from Cormac Mac Art, held 4
domirzant position round Lough Erne from the fourteenth century untj
the breaking up of the tribal system under the Tudors. From about
1302 to 1608 they ruled Fermanagh, each chief being inaugurated o
Cuil Cagh Mountain; they possessed four strong castles, and, in the
reign of Edward III. had a fleet oni Lough Erne. Edward II. summoned
the Maguire of his time to the invasion of Scotland. The rule of Sir
John Perrott initiated the steps which finally broke their power. The
O’Donnells had supported the Maguires against rebellious sub-clans, but,
the Elizabethan policy of ‘‘shiring”’ the tribal territories introduced a
new factor. When I'itzwilliam first proposed to appoint a Sheriff of
Fermanagh Maguire courteously enquired (in case of accidents) exactly
what the ‘‘eric’’ (blood-price) of a Sheriff was reckoned to be. ‘But
Perrott divided the clan by placmg under the protection’ of the Crown,
a rival claimant to the chieftainship, whose line became known as ‘“The =
- English Maguires.”” Hugh Maguire, ‘‘ The Irish Maguire,’’ joined Hugh
O’Neill in his march into Munster, and was mortally wounded 1n a com- 53
bat with St. Leger, whom he killed. His successor joined" the Flight of
the Karls, and died at Genoa. The ‘‘Irish Maguires,” who retained some
of thelr land, lost their position when the head of the fa.mlly was Lllled L
in 1691 serving under St. Ruth. Meanwhile the ‘English Maguires,” {2
who were granted a peerage, had joined in the rebellion of 1641, and
Lord Maguire, whose plan for seizing Dublin, failed, was executed in

- 1641. Tlli‘s lJranc,h of the family also remained true to the Stuarts, and :
contrlbuﬁ’etf many soldiers to the Irish Brigade. The lecturer ended by’
